name: Scarab Amulet
description: The Scarab Amulet, a potent symbol in ancient Egyptian mythology, represents rebirth, renewal, and protection. Modelled after the scarab beetle (Scarabaeus sacer), which was observed rolling dung balls across the ground (analogous to the sun's journey across the sky), the amulet embodies the concept of Khepri, the god of the rising sun. Egyptians believed the scarab spontaneously generated life from inert matter, mirroring the idea of resurrection and eternal life. These amulets were often placed over the heart of the deceased during mummification, acting as a 'heart scarab.' The heart scarab served as a plea to the heart (considered the seat of intelligence and memory) not to testify against the deceased during the Weighing of the Heart ceremony in the afterlife. Crafted from materials like faience, carnelian, lapis lazuli, or even precious metals, scarab amulets frequently bore inscriptions from the Book of the Dead, further enhancing their protective and transformative powers. They were believed to guide the soul safely through the underworld, ensuring rebirth and immortality. The scarab's association with the sun also connected it to Ra, the sun god, reinforcing its power to bring light and ward off darkness. The amulet served as a constant reminder of the cycle of life, death, and rebirth, a core tenet of Egyptian beliefs.
subtitle: Symbol of Rebirth and Protection
origin: Inspired by the scarab beetle's behavior, associated with the god Khepri.
currentLocation: Various museums and private collections worldwide.
associatedHeroes: Various pharaohs and individuals seeking afterlife

keyMyths.title: The Heart Scarab
keyMyths.description: Heart scarabs were placed on mummies to prevent the heart from betraying the deceased during judgment. It ensured passage into the afterlife.

keyMyths.title: Khepri and the Sun
keyMyths.description: The scarab beetle was associated with Khepri, the god who rolled the sun across the sky each morning. This symbolizes daily rebirth.

sources.author: E.A. Wallis Budge
sources.description: A collection of spells and rituals intended to guide the deceased through the afterlife.
sources.text: A collection of spells and rituals intended to guide the deceased through the afterlife.

sources.author: James P. Allen
